Project description: This is an end to end automated performance analysis project of A&E attendances and  Emergency admissions of NHS UK. The ETL pipeline is created to automatically extract data from the NHS website on a monthly basis, which is processed using python, stored in SQL lite database and loaded into PowerBI for performance analysis
Project content:
PROJECT OVERVIEW
This project is an end to end automated data analytics pipeline to analyse A&E attendences and emergency admissions data of NHS UK.

The project involves automatic monthly extraction of publicly available NHS yearly and monthly datasets by scraping, processing the data using python, uploading them into SQL lite database in a star schema, loads it into a dynamic interactive Power BI dashboard for analysis of performance metrics.

The dashboard helps in analysis of historical trends in A&E performance, patient load, performance metrics/KPIs measuring service level of NHS regions and organisations alike and helps in understanding the current pressure and identifying organisations with scope for improvement.

 OBJECTIVES

1. Build an automated ETL pipeline for healthcare data analysis
2. Design a star schema data model suitable for analytics.
3. Perform trend analysis of A&E attendances.
4. Track Month-over-Month (MoM) and Year-over-Year (YoY) performance.
5. Visualise hospital demand and performance metrics using Power BI.
6. Identify performance levels of NHS regions and organisations

 Tech Stack
 VS Code:
Coding
 Python:
Web scraping, Data ingestion and ETL pipeline
 Pandas:
Data transformation and cleaning
 SQLite
Data warehouse storage
 SQL 
Star schema modelling and querying
 Power BI
Interactive dashboard and data visualisation connected to SQL Database

 Data Source

Public NHS datasets from NHS England covering monthly Accident & Emergency attendance statistics : https://www.england.nhs.uk/

 Data Pipeline Architecture

1. Data Extraction - Monthly NHS datasets are scraped from website automatically and combined using Python
2. Data Processing - Data is cleaned and standardised using Python and Pandas.
3. Database Loading - Processed data is loaded into a SQLite database
4. Data Modelling - Star schema is implemented with dimension tables (dim_date; dim_organisation) and fact tables (fact_nhs_booked_attendence; fact_nhs_unbooked_attendence) to enable time series and   organisational performance based analysis
5. Visualization - Power BI connects directly to the SQLite database to create an analytics dashboard.

 Key Metrics

Total A&E Attendances ; Month-over-Month (MoM) Change; Year-over-Year (YoY) Change; 4-hour waiting time breaches; Emergency admissions

 Dashboard Features

KPI cards for cumulative upto date and latest month attendance metrics; Attendance trend analysis; Organisation performance drill-down; Hospital pressure comparison

 Automation

The pipeline is designed to for scheduled run monthly, with data updated automatically when new datasets are released. The analytics dashboard also gets updated automatically on refresh.

 Project Structure

├── data/
│   ├── raw

│

│   ├── process_data.py

│   └── pipeline.py
│
├── sql/

│
├── dashboard/
│   └── nhs_portfolio.pbix
│ 
├── Images/
│   ├── Dashboard screenshot_1.png
│   └── Dashboard preview_2.png
│
├── run_pipeline.bat
├── README.md
└── requirements.txt

 Insights and recommendations
1. High proportion of patients breaching the 4-hour target :
About 25.83% of A&E attendances exceeded the 4-hour wait target (vs NHS standard of 95% within 4 hours), showing system pressure despite slight improvement from 2024 to 2025.
Recommendation: Improve patient flow and implement real-time operational dashboards for capacity monitoring, planning or staff reorganisation.

2. Seasonal demand drives waiting time:
Waiting times increase between August and January, indicating higher pressure linked to seasonal illnesses in winter and higher emergency demand.
Recommendation: Use predictive demand forecasting and increase winter staffing, bed capacity and community support.

3. Type 1 emergency departments drive most delays:
Type 1 departments handle 61% of attendances but account for 95% of >4-hour waits, highlighting congestion in major emergency departments.
Recommendation: Can consider diversion to urgent treatment centres (Type 3) and expand capacity in high-pressure departments

4. Long admission delays (>12 hours) are increasing:
Around 2% of patients face >12-hour delays from decision to admit to admission, indicating downstream hospital bed and capacity constraints.
Recommendation: Improve discharge planning, bed management and hospital flow systems to reduce admission bottlenecks.

5. Performance varies significantly across regions and organisations:
Some low volume regions show higher waiting percentages, while certain high volume organisations maintain better performance, suggesting operational efficiency differences.
Recommendation: Benchmark high-performing trusts, analyse staffing and resource allocation and implement targeted performance improvement strate

Project description: A Customer Churn Prediction Model for a Subscription-Based Online Business in the Hospitality Sector
Project content: Customer Churn Prediction & Segmentation for a Subscription Platform - End-to-End Customer Analytics Project | SQL · Python · Machine Learning · Power BI Confidentiality Note This project is based on a real-world analytics consultancy engagement. All company identifiers, sensitive data, and proprietary business information have been removed or anonymised. The repository focuses solely on the analytical methodology, modelling approach and insights generation process. And hence only limited details of business performance and improvement recommendations are presented here. Project Overview Customer retention is a critical challenge for subscription-based platforms where sustainable growth depends on balancing customer acquisition with long-term engagement. This project analyses customer behavioural and transactional data to identify the drivers of churn and develop a predictive analytics framework for proactive retention strategies. The analysis combines customer segmentation techniques with machine learning models to generate actionable insights for improving customer lifetime value. The project was conducted using real-world subscription data, with all sensitive information anonymised for confidentiality. Business Problem Subscription businesses frequently experience high customer churn, which directly impacts revenue stability and long-term growth. This project addresses the following key business questions: 1. What behavioural patterns distinguish retained customers from churned customers? 2. Are discount campaigns effective in improving long-term customer retention? 3. How can customer segmentation be used to guide retention strategies? 4. Can machine learning models accurately predict customers at risk of churn? Project Objectives 1. Analyse customer transaction and engagement data to identify behavioural patterns. 2. Evaluate the impact of discount campaigns on customer retention. 3. Develop customer segmentation using RFM analysis and clustering techniques. 4. Build predictive machine learning models to forecast customer churn. 5. Provide data-driven business recommendations for improving customer retention strategies. Tech Stack 1. Data Processing & Analysis - Python (Pandas, NumPy), SQL 2. Machine Learning - Scikit-learn, Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest 3. Data Visualisation - Power BI, Matplotlib / Seaborn 4. Development Environment - Jupyter Notebook Analytics Workflow The project follows a structured analytics pipeline: 1. Data Extraction a. Customer and subscription data extracted using SQL queries. 2. Data Cleaning & Preparation a. Handling missing values through cross-table reconciliation and selective imputation. b. Outlier analysis while preserving meaningful behavioural extremes. c. Creation of analytical datasets for segmentation and modelling. 3. Feature Engineering a. Construction of behavioural metrics including: - Customer tenure - Billing frequency - Transaction types - Discount usage b. Development of RFM-style metrics adapted for subscription engagement. 4. Customer Segmentation a. RFM scoring applied to capture engagement patterns. b. K-means clustering used to identify distinct customer groups. c. Optimal clusters determined using elbow method and silhouette scores. 5. Predictive Modelling a. Churn prediction models tested: - Logistic Regression - Decision Tree - Random Forest b. Model performance evaluated using: - Accuracy - Recall - F1 Score 6. Customer Risk Profiling Customers categorised into low, medium, and high churn risk groups. 7. Business Insights & Recommendations Analytical findings translated into actionable strategies for retention optimisation. General methodology workflow Key Insights 1. Customer churn is strongly associated with tenure, billing behaviour, and discount usage, indicating behavioural engagement is a major retention driver. 2. Broad discount campaigns tend to generate short-term acquisition but can attract high-churn customers with lower lifetime value. 3. Customer segmentation revealed distinct behavioural cohorts including high-value loyal users, casual users, and dormant customers. 4. The Random Forest model demonstrated the best predictive performance, enabling early identification of customers at risk of churn. 5. Integrating segmentation with predictive modelling provides a more holistic understanding of customer behaviour and attrition risk. Business Recommendations 1. Shift from broad discount campaigns to targeted promotions focused on at-risk segments. 2. Implement personalised engagement strategies for high-value loyal customers to maximise retention. 3. Use predictive churn scores to trigger proactive retention interventions within CRM w

Project description: This project develops a machine learning model to predict road accident severity in the UK using publicly available road safety data. The objective is to support insurance risk assessment and data-driven premium pricing by identifying factors that influence accident severity.
Project content:
UK Road Accident Severity Prediction for Insurance Risk Assessment (Machine Learning Project)

 Introduction
The main aim of this Python based Machine Learning project is to use historical UK road safety data, particularly of the year 2023, published by the UK department of transport (UK DoT), to support the premium pricing based on risk assessment for a CAR INSURANCE COMPANY.
The primary objective of this project is to build a machine learning model to efficiently predict the severity of accident a car driver is likely to be involved in by assessing influencial pre-known variables related to driver and vehicle characteristics simulating a real life scenario based on learnings from historical accident data. I intend to use the accidents and vehicle data for the year 2023 pubished by the UK DoT which has details of accidents like accident severity, date of accident, area classification in which accident has taken place, the driver's demographics & geodemographics - age, sex, distance banding , home area type of driver, the vehicle characteristics such as model, engine capacity, propulsion code, etc.These are the potentially known variables during an insurance application process.
Understanding the influential driver and vehicle characteristics over severity of accidents will help in better segmentation of customers. Thereby, insurance companies can competitively tailor premiums, and also mitigate potential risk to the company.
This approach enhances underwriting decisions, promotes fair pricing, and supports data-driven risk management strategies. 
The final output will be a machine learning model capable of classifying or estimating the severity of potential accidents a driver may be involved in, based on information available at the point of application.
This project uses publicly available road safety data insights for practical applications in the automotive insurance sector serving as a proactive tool for risk assessment.
The merged dataset contains over 165,000 records.

 Data Source 

https://www.gov.uk/government/statistical-data-sets/road-safety-open-data 

 Data Preparation and Feature Engineering

Extensive preprocessing and feature engineering were performed to prepare the dataset for machine learning.
Key steps included:
1. Data integration: Accident and vehicle datasets merged using accident_index.
2. Data cleaning: Unknown values coded as -1 were converted to missing values and handled using imputation techniques.
3. Imputation techniques: Median imputation, KNN imputation, and MICE (Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations) were used to address missing values.
4. Outlier detection: Outliers in variables such as engine_capacity_cc, age_of_vehicle, and age_of_driver were identified using boxplots and removed using the IQR method.
5. Feature transformation: Log transformation applied to skewed variables to improve distribution.
6. Category consolidation: Low-frequency categories were grouped to improve statistical stability.
7.Feature encoding: Vehicle brand information was extracted from model names and encoded for modelling.
A stratified subset of 10,000 records was used for modelling to maintain representative distributions while enabling efficient experimentation.

 Exploratory Data Analysis

Exploratory analysis identified patterns in accident severity and informed feature engineering.
Key observations included:
1. Accident severity is highly imbalanced, with minor accidents accounting for ~80% of cases.
2. Numerical variables such as engine capacity and vehicle age are right-skewed.
3. Certain features such as driver age, vehicle characteristics, and urban/rural location show relationships with accident severity.
4. Feature importance analysis using Chi-square tests and correlation analysis identified the most influential predictors.
5. Top predictive features included:
      a) Urban vs rural road type
      b) Journey purpose of driver
      c) Engine capacity
      d) Vehicle age
      e) Driver travel distance band
      f)Encoded car brand

 Machine Learning Modelling

Multiple classification models were trained to predict accident severity levels.
Models implemented were:
1. Dummy Classifier (baseline)
2. Logistic Regression
3. Decision Tree
4. Random Forest

To address severe class imbalance, SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) was applied to balance the training data. Hyperparameter tuning was conducted using GridSearchCV and Bayesian optimisation, with 10-fold cross-validation used for model evaluation.

 Model Performance

Model performance was evaluated using accuracy and macro F1-score, with F1-score prioritised due to the imbalanced class distribution.
Key results are :
1. Baseline Model: Achieved high accuracy (79.8%) but a low f1-score (0.296), indicating poor detection of minority classes
2. Logistic Regression: Performed similarly to the baseline on unbalanced data. SMOTE balancing improved the f1-score slightly but led to inconsistent performance across clas
